lines: I thank you. I am not of many words, but I
thank you.
fullText: I thank you. I am not of many words, but I thank you.

lines: There is no measure in the occasion that
breeds. Therefore the sadness is without limit.
fullText: There is no measure in the occasion that breeds. Therefore the sadness is without limit.

lines: And when I have heard it, what blessing
brings it?
fullText: And when I have heard it, what blessing brings it?

lines: I wonder that thou, being, as thou sayst thou
art, born under Saturn, goest about to apply a moral
medicine to a mortifying mischief. I cannot hide
what I am. I must be sad when I have cause, and
smile at no man's jests; eat when I have stomach,
and wait for no man's leisure; sleep when I am
drowsy, and tend on no man's business; laugh when
I am merry, and claw no man in his humor.
fullText: I wonder that thou, being, as thou sayst thou art, born under Saturn, goest about to apply a moral medicine to a mortifying mischief. I cannot hide what I am. I must be sad when I have cause, and smile at no man's jests; eat when I have stomach, and wait for no man's leisure; sleep when I am drowsy, and tend on no man's business; laugh when I am merry, and claw no man in his humor.

lines: I had rather be a canker in a hedge than a
rose in his grace, and it better fits my blood to be
disdained of all than to fashion a carriage to rob
love from any. In this, though I cannot be said to be
a flattering honest man, it must not be denied but I
am a plain-dealing villain. I am trusted with a
muzzle and enfranchised with a clog; therefore I
have decreed not to sing in my cage. If I had my
mouth, I would bite; if I had my liberty, I would do
my liking. In the meantime, let me be that I am, and
seek not to alter me.
fullText: I had rather be a canker in a hedge than a rose in his grace, and it better fits my blood to be disdained of all than to fashion a carriage to rob love from any. In this, though I cannot be said to be a flattering honest man, it must not be denied but I am a plain-dealing villain. I am trusted with a muzzle and enfranchised with a clog; therefore I have decreed not to sing in my cage. If I had my mouth, I would bite; if I had my liberty, I would do my liking. In the meantime, let me be that I am, and seek not to alter me.

lines: I make all use of it, for I use it only. Who
comes here?
fullText: I make all use of it, for I use it only. Who comes here?

lines: Will it serve for any model to build mischief
on? What is he for a fool that betroths himself to
unquietness?
fullText: Will it serve for any model to build mischief on? What is he for a fool that betroths himself to unquietness?

lines: Who, the most exquisite Claudio?
fullText: Who, the most exquisite Claudio?

lines: A proper squire. And who, and who? Which
way looks he?
fullText: A proper squire. And who, and who? Which way looks he?

lines: A very forward March chick! How came you
to this?
fullText: A very forward March chick! How came you to this?

lines: Come, come, let us thither. This may prove
food to my displeasure. That young start-up hath
all the glory of my overthrow. If I can cross him any
way, I bless myself every way. You are both sure, and
will assist me?
fullText: Come, come, let us thither. This may prove food to my displeasure. That young start-up hath all the glory of my overthrow. If I can cross him any way, I bless myself every way. You are both sure, and will assist me?

lines: Let us to the great supper. Their cheer is the
greater that I am subdued. Would the cook were o'
my mind! Shall we go prove what's to be done?
fullText: Let us to the great supper. Their cheer is the greater that I am subdued. Would the cook were o' my mind! Shall we go prove what's to be done?

lines: Sure my brother is amorous
on Hero, and hath withdrawn her father to break
with him about it. The ladies follow her, and but one
visor remains.
fullText: Sure my brother is amorous on Hero, and hath withdrawn her father to break with him about it. The ladies follow her, and but one visor remains.

lines: Are not you Signior Benedick?
fullText: Are not you Signior Benedick?

lines: Signior, you are very near my brother in his
love. He is enamored on Hero. I pray you dissuade
him from her. She is no equal for his birth. You
may do the part of an honest man in it.
fullText: Signior, you are very near my brother in his love. He is enamored on Hero. I pray you dissuade him from her. She is no equal for his birth. You may do the part of an honest man in it.

lines: I heard him swear his affection.
fullText: I heard him swear his affection.

lines: Come, let us to the banquet.
fullText: Come, let us to the banquet.

lines: It is so. The Count Claudio shall marry the
daughter of Leonato.
fullText: It is so. The Count Claudio shall marry the daughter of Leonato.

lines: Any bar, any cross, any impediment will be
med'cinable to me. I am sick in displeasure to him,
and whatsoever comes athwart his affection ranges
evenly with mine. How canst thou cross this
marriage?
fullText: Any bar, any cross, any impediment will be med'cinable to me. I am sick in displeasure to him, and whatsoever comes athwart his affection ranges evenly with mine. How canst thou cross this marriage?

lines: Show me briefly how.
fullText: Show me briefly how.

lines: I remember.
fullText: I remember.

lines: What life is in that to be the death of this
marriage?
fullText: What life is in that to be the death of this marriage?

lines: What proof shall I make of that?
fullText: What proof shall I make of that?

lines: Only to despite them I will endeavor
anything.
fullText: Only to despite them I will endeavor anything.

lines: Grow this to what adverse issue it can, I will
put it in practice. Be cunning in the working this,
and thy fee is a thousand ducats.
fullText: Grow this to what adverse issue it can, I will put it in practice. Be cunning in the working this, and thy fee is a thousand ducats.

lines: I will presently go learn their day of
marriage.
fullText: I will presently go learn their day of marriage.

lines: My lord and brother, God save you.
fullText: My lord and brother, God save you.

lines: If your leisure served, I would speak with
you.
fullText: If your leisure served, I would speak with you.

lines: If it please you. Yet Count Claudio may
hear, for what I would speak of concerns him.
fullText: If it please you. Yet Count Claudio may hear, for what I would speak of concerns him.

lines: Means your Lordship to be
married tomorrow?
fullText: Means your Lordship to be married tomorrow?

lines: I know not that, when he knows what I
know.
fullText: I know not that, when he knows what I know.

lines: You may think I love you not. Let that
appear hereafter, and aim better at me by that I
now will manifest. For my brother, I think he holds
you well, and in dearness of heart hath holp to effect
your ensuing marriage--surely suit ill spent and
labor ill bestowed.
fullText: You may think I love you not. Let that appear hereafter, and aim better at me by that I now will manifest. For my brother, I think he holds you well, and in dearness of heart hath holp to effect your ensuing marriage--surely suit ill spent and labor ill bestowed.

lines: I came hither to tell you; and, circumstances
shortened, for she has been too long
a-talking of, the lady is disloyal.
fullText: I came hither to tell you; and, circumstances shortened, for she has been too long a-talking of, the lady is disloyal.

lines: Even she: Leonato's Hero, your Hero, every
man's Hero.
fullText: Even she: Leonato's Hero, your Hero, every man's Hero.

lines: The word is too good to paint out her
wickedness. I could say she were worse. Think you
of a worse title, and I will fit her to it. Wonder not
till further warrant. Go but with me tonight, you
shall see her chamber window entered, even the
night before her wedding day. If you love her then,
tomorrow wed her. But it would better fit your
honor to change your mind.
fullText: The word is too good to paint out her wickedness. I could say she were worse. Think you of a worse title, and I will fit her to it. Wonder not till further warrant. Go but with me tonight, you shall see her chamber window entered, even the night before her wedding day. If you love her then, tomorrow wed her. But it would better fit your honor to change your mind.

lines: If you dare not trust that you see, confess
not that you know. If you will follow me, I will
show you enough, and when you have seen more
and heard more, proceed accordingly.
fullText: If you dare not trust that you see, confess not that you know. If you will follow me, I will show you enough, and when you have seen more and heard more, proceed accordingly.

lines: I will disparage her no farther till you are
my witnesses. Bear it coldly but till midnight, and
let the issue show itself.
fullText: I will disparage her no farther till you are my witnesses. Bear it coldly but till midnight, and let the issue show itself.

lines: O plague right well prevented! So will you
say when you have seen the sequel.
fullText: O plague right well prevented! So will you say when you have seen the sequel.

lines: Sir, they are spoken, and these things are true.
fullText: Sir, they are spoken, and these things are true.

lines: Fie, fie, they are not to be named, my lord,
Not to be spoke of!
There is not chastity enough in language,
Without offense, to utter them.--Thus, pretty lady,
I am sorry for thy much misgovernment.
fullText: Fie, fie, they are not to be named, my lord, Not to be spoke of! There is not chastity enough in language, Without offense, to utter them.--Thus, pretty lady, I am sorry for thy much misgovernment.

lines: Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light,
Smother her spirits up.
fullText: Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light, Smother her spirits up.
